questions.question: By default, where is the Notion desktop app installed, and is it set to update automatically?

options.text: In a random user folder, with automatic updates disabled.
options.explanation: The default installation is in the /Applications folder, and it is configured to update automatically with each new release.

options.text: In the /Applications folder, with automatic updates disabled.
options.explanation: While installed in /Applications by default, the app is configured to update automatically with each new release.

options.text: In the /Applications folder, with automatic updates enabled.
options.explanation: The default location is the /Applications folder, and the app is set to update automatically with each new release.

options.text: In the ~/Applications folder, with automatic updates enabled.
options.explanation: The default location is /Applications, not ~/Applications.

questions.question: When installing Notion on a shared machine, which option allows users to update the app independently without needing administrator privileges?

options.text: Installing in the /Applications folder.
options.explanation: Installing in the /Applications folder requires users to be administrators to update the app.

options.text: Installing in the ~/Applications folder.
options.explanation: Installing in the ~/Applications folder gives each user their own installation, allowing them to update without admin rights.

options.text: Using the Universal build instead of an Intel build.
options.explanation: The build type (Universal, Intel, or Apple Silicon) affects compatibility, not the permission requirements for updating.

options.text: Disabling automatic updates entirely.
options.explanation: Disabling automatic updates requires a terminal command and does not grant general file write permissions to users.

questions.question: What is a characteristic of installing Notion in the /Applications folder on a shared machine?

options.text: Updates made to Notion apply to all users on the machine.
options.explanation: The article states that updates made to Notion apply to all users on the machine when installed in /Applications.

options.text: Each user has their own separate installation of Notion.
options.explanation: Each user having their own installation is a characteristic of installing in the ~/Applications folder, not /Applications.

options.text: Users do not need to be administrators to update the app.
options.explanation: Users must be administrators to update Notion when it is installed in the /Applications folder.

options.text: Updates do not affect other instances of the app for other users.
options.explanation: Because it is a shared installation, updates apply to all users on the machine.

questions.question: Which terminal command should be run to disable automatic updates for a specific user?

options.text: defaults write notion.id NotionNoAutoUpdates -bool NO
options.explanation: This command is used to re-enable automatic updates, not to disable them.

options.text: defaults write notion.id NotionNoAutoUpdates -bool YES
options.explanation: Setting the value to 'YES' prevents Notion from automatically checking for updates.

options.text: defaults write notion.id NotionNoAutoUpdates -bool FALSE
options.explanation: Notion uses 'YES' to disable and 'NO' to enable. 'FALSE' is not the correct boolean string used by this command.

options.text: defaults write notion.id NotionDisableUpdates -bool YES
options.explanation: The specific key name is 'NotionNoAutoUpdates', not 'NotionDisableUpdates'.

questions.question: When distributing the Notion desktop app to employees, which build should you choose for a mixed environment of Intel and Apple Silicon Macs?

options.text: Intel (x64) build.
options.explanation: The Intel build will only run on Intel-based Macs and will not work on Apple Silicon machines.

options.text: Apple Silicon (arm64) build.
options.explanation: The Apple Silicon build will only run on Apple Silicon machines and will not work on Intel machines.

options.text: Universal (x64 or arm64) build.
options.explanation: The Universal build is designed to run on both Intel and Apple Silicon machines.

options.text: A custom build created by IT administrators.
options.explanation: The article lists only three official builds: Intel, Apple Silicon, and Universal.

questions.question: What is a specific characteristic of the Universal build mentioned in the article?

options.text: It is the smallest file size among the available builds.
options.explanation: The Universal build is significantly larger than the other builds because it contains code for both architectures.

options.text: It does not work on shared machines.
options.explanation: The Universal build works on both Intel and Apple Silicon machines, and can be used on shared machines.

options.text: It is much larger than the other builds.
options.explanation: The article notes that the Universal build is much larger because it includes code for both Intel and Apple Silicon.

options.text: It requires a separate download for every user on a shared machine.
options.explanation: The build size is a file size characteristic, not a download requirement per user.

questions.question: Which command is used to set ownership and permissions for a permissions group on a subfolder of /Applications?

options.text: chown -R :group /Applications/subfolder && chmod -R 755 /Applications/subfolder
options.explanation: While `chown` is correct, `chmod 755` only allows read and execute for the group, not write access. The correct permission is 775.

options.text: chown -R :group /Applications/subfolder && chmod -R 775 /Applications/subfolder
options.explanation: This command correctly changes the group owner and sets read, write, and execute permissions for the group (775).

options.text: chown -R :group /Applications/subfolder && chmod -R 770 /Applications/subfolder
options.explanation: While `chown` is correct, `chmod 770` restricts write access to the owner only, preventing the group from writing files.

options.text: chown -R :group /Applications/subfolder && chmod -R 777 /Applications/subfolder
options.explanation: While `chmod 777` allows write access, it is generally considered insecure because it allows 'world' write access, whereas 775 restricts it to the group.

questions.question: Why does the article recommend installing Notion in a subfolder of /Applications and using a permissions group?

options.text: To ensure that only one user can see the app at a time.
options.explanation: The recommendation is about permissions, not visibility or exclusive access.

options.text: To allow non-admin users to update the app independently.
options.explanation: By setting group ownership and write permissions, users within the group can update the app without requiring individual administrator privileges.

options.text: To prevent automatic updates from ever occurring.
options.explanation: Installing in a subfolder and using a group does not disable updates; it just manages who can perform them.

options.text: To reduce the file size of the installation package.
options.explanation: The file size is determined by the build (Intel/Universal), not the installation path.

questions.question: If you want to test a new version of Notion before rolling it out to everyone, what feature would you use?

options.text: The Intel build specifically.
options.explanation: Testing versions usually involves managing update settings, not selecting specific CPU architectures.

options.text: Disabling automatic updates.
options.explanation: Disabling automatic updates allows IT admins to control the rollout timing and test new versions before a widespread deployment.

options.text: Installing in the ~/Applications folder.
options.explanation: Installing in ~/Applications affects permissions, not the timing of version rollout.

options.text: Disabling automatic updates for the entire company.
options.explanation: The feature allows you to disable updates for individual users to control the rollout, not necessarily for the entire company at once.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `defaults write` command mentioned in the article?

options.text: To change the installation path of the app.
options.explanation: The `defaults` command is used to modify system settings regarding auto-updates, not to move the application binary itself.

options.text: To disable or re-enable automatic update checking.
options.explanation: The `defaults write` command is used to toggle the `NotionNoAutoUpdates` setting to YES or NO.

options.text: To grant user write permissions to the app folder.
options.explanation: Granting write permissions is done using the `chown` and `chmod` commands, not `defaults`.

options.text: To download the latest version of the app.
options.explanation: Downloading and updating is handled by the app's built-in updater, which is controlled by these defaults settings.
